local_news.findings: The City of Edmonds will launch a major sewer and storm-pipe rehabilitation project on May 4. The 2025 CIPP (Cured-in-Place Pipe) program will repair or replace failing lines at 27 locations identified in a 2013 review. Work will be phased so no neighborhood loses service for more than a few hours and will take roughly six weeks to complete.

City staff unveiled the 60 % design for Stage 3 of the Highway 99 Revitalization Project at an April 24 open house. The segment between 238th and 244th Streets SW will add buffered bike lanes, new sidewalks, LED streetlights, rain gardens and an improved bus stop. Public comment is being accepted through May 9; construction is slated for 2027.

The Washington State Court of Appeals reopened the long-running “Rimmer tree mandate” case. After dismissing the suit as moot earlier this month, the court granted homeowner Nathan Rimmer’s motion for reconsideration and gave Edmonds 15 days to respond. The revived litigation could still expose the city to damages for its since-repealed tree-replacement rule.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has wrapped 2025-26 auditions, but email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the August 2026 list for all voice parts; rehearsals are Tue 6:30-9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish (1805 38th Ave, Seattle). Choral Arts Northwest will hold 2026-27 auditions in July/Aug—contact Miriam Anderson (miriam@choralartsnw.org) for scheduling. Seattle Choral Company typically schedules July/Aug sessions at Phinney Ridge Lutheran; watch seattlechoralcompany.org for the next announcement.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries runs weekly Toddler Storytimes at Lynnwood (Mon 9:30 am) and Edmonds branches; doors open 9:15-9:35 am for pre-opening sessions. Bonus: Imagine Children’s Museum brings Little Science Lab to Lynnwood Library on Mon May 11, 2-2:30 pm—hands-on STEM play for ages 18 mo-3 yr. Check sno-isle.org/events for full May calendars and registration links.

intellectual_journals.findings: Ian Penman explores the Beach Boys' music as a form of secular gospel, arguing that Brian Wilson's 1966 Pet Sounds achieves a cathedral-like transcendence through three-minute pop songs. Penman positions Wilson alongside Monteverdi, noting that tracks such as "Don't Talk (Put Your Head on My Shoulder)" and "God Only Knows" create sacred space without religiosity. He traces a moral arc from the innocent "In My Room" (1963) to the existential "'Til I Die" (1971), framing the band's catalogue as a spiritual autobiography shadowed by villains like Manson and Spector. The essay resists hagiography, [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy revisits Proust's 1905 essay "On Reading" to defend literature as a technology of personal freedom rather than moral improvement. Rejecting Ruskin's utilitarian view that books must "pay" by making us better, Champy follows Proust in claiming that reading's value lies in preserving fugitive sensations and expanding our inner life. She argues that literature offers a uniquely disinterested communion with other minds, unburdened by social performance. The essay warns against market-driven metrics for culture, insisting that the slow, solitary act of reading trains a kind of secular  [TRUNCATED]

Will Alden reports from an Easter Sunday picnic in Los Angeles where the exiled Constitutionalist Party of Iran rallied around Reza Pahlavi as a "transitional" leader awaiting Tehran's collapse. Amid EDM tracks titled "Ayatollah Is Dead," diaspora monarchists chant for Trump and Pahlavi, selling posters that thank Netanyahu and Trump for the ongoing war. Alden details the generational split between patrician exiles who fled 1979 and younger hardliners advocating violent restoration, noting how LA's "Tehrangeles" has become a staging ground for Republican foreign-policy influence. The piece cap [TRUNCATED]

A joint investigation with FRONTLINE documents how ICE and CBP under Trump have arrested more than 300 US citizens during immigration protests, only to see the cases repeatedly collapse. Reviewing body-cam and bystander video, reporters find officers fabricated claims of assault or conspiracy; over one-third of prosecutions were dismissed or lost at trial. The pattern reveals a deliberate strategy to chill dissent through process punishment: even when charges fail, protestors spend months in legal limbo, draining resources and deterring future demonstrations. The report focuses on Alejandro Or [TRUNCATED]

In a lyrical meditation, Maria Popova reframes Walt Whitman's lifelong revision of Leaves of Grass as a field guide to becoming oneself. She follows the teenage Whitman—apprentice printer, ferry commuter, Bowery Theatre regular—who vowed to "compose a wonderful and ponderous book" that would merge body and soul, nation and cosmos. Popova reads Whitman's endless re-issuing of the same poems as an existential practice: each republication was an act of self-overcoming, an invitation to readers to likewise "re-examine all you have been told." The essay positions Whitman's contradictions—democrat a [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Matteo Raffaelli's "Triertium in Modern Theology" (Modern Theology, 2026) advances a new Trinitarian ontology rooted in Czech pansophy, positioning Erwin Schadel's relational thought within the TRIERTIUM Research Group's transdisciplinary framework. Christopher Isabelle's "Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field" (PhilArchive, 2026) argues that dyadic structures are insufficient for emergence—only a triadic relational field, actively maintained through tension, can sustain intelligibility across primordial separation. Trent Slade's "A Triadic Ontology with SU(3) Invariants" (Zenodo, 2025) operationalizes triadic logic via qutrit-based dynamics, using SU(3) symmetry to construct a minimal ontic kernel for non-linear triadic systems. Finally, the April 2026 Zenodo thesis "The Triadic Closure of Existence" formulates a physics-level ontology where binding, transformation, and relational readout co-emerge as non-negotiable closure roles, replacing linear [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous research landscape in 2026 has crystallized around three converging breakthroughs. Google Cloud's PaperOrchestra deploys a five-agent ensemble that converts raw lab notes into submission-ready LaTeX manuscripts—complete with literature reviews, generated figures, and API-verified citations—averaging 6,070 LLM calls per paper. Running in parallel, the Cambridge astrophysics team’s Cmbagent system (powering the Denario AI Scientist) just won the FAIR Universe Weak Lensing Challenge by orchestrating idea generation, code execution, and iterative pipeline refinement; human-in-the-loop steering lifted its autonomous runs from sub-expert to first-place performance. Meanwhile, LangChain’s March 2026 Deep Agents framework introduces production-grade abstractions for long-running, crash-recoverable workflows: persistent memory, sub-agent delegation, and MongoDB-backed checkpointing that let internal coding agents fix bugs across repositories over days, not seconds. Prompt enginee [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congress accelerates UAP-disclosure drive as President Trump orders release of classified files. The House Oversight Task Force, led by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, demanded 46 UAP videos from the Pentagon by April 14—deadline missed, prompting accusations of deliberate obstruction. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth confirms AARO’s caseload exceeds 2,000 unresolved cases. The White House registered alien.gov and aliens.gov domains ahead of imminent data drops. AARO’s September 2025 guidance clarifies declassification hurdles: most imagery remains classified to protect sensor capabilities, not the phenomena themselves. Meanwhile, former intelligence officials testify UAPs have “split aircraft formations,” and physicist Avi Loeb urges release of raw, unprocessed data for scientific scrutiny. Schumer’s UAP Disclosure Act amendment, embedded in the FY26 NDAA, mandates a centralized National Archives collection and presumption of immediate disclosure for all federal UAP records, including non-human [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The New Yorker
newyorker.dek: A new restaurant in the West Village serves only dishes made from ingredients grown or foraged within a five-mile radius.
newyorker.text: Last week, on a drizzly Monday evening, the chef Ivy Chen stood in the doorway of her new West Village restaurant, Forage & Fire, greeting the first guests to sample a menu composed entirely of ingredients sourced within a five-mile radius of Manhattan. Chen, who previously helmed the Michelin-starred Willow in Brooklyn, has traded the precision of imported truffles and dry-aged beef for a hyper-local experiment: ramps from Inwood Hill Park, sea lettuce harvested off Pier 40, and venison from a cull in Staten Island’s Greenbelt. The dining room, once a candle-lit wine bar, now glows with Edison bulbs and the occasional crackle of the open hearth where Chen sears sassafras-smoked duck. Diners sat at reclaimed oak tables, sipping spruce-tip gin cocktails while debating whether the policy against olive oil (Italy is, after all, 4,200 miles away) constituted culinary heresy. Chen, unfazed, argued that constraint breeds creativity: “If I can’t get it by bike or ferry, it doesn’t belong on the plate.” The prix-fixe tasting menu—seven courses, $148—sold out within hours of release, though some guests grumbled that the portions felt more Brooklyn backyard than Manhattan splurge. Still, the roasted knotweed with pickled knotweed was a revelation, and the service, led by servers who double as foraging guides, charmed even the skeptics. By dessert—a wildflower honey custard with candied violets—most had conceded that the five-mile rule might just be the most New York thing to happen to dining since the cronut.

enriched_articles.title: Waiting for Day Zero: Los Angeles Iranians and the Crown Prince
enriched_articles.text: In Los Angeles, home to the largest population of Iranians outside Iran, supporters of the exiled crown prince insist that their moment has come. The Constitutionalist Party of Iran (CPI) has been organizing weekly rallies where thousands chant for a "free Iran" and hold signs supporting both Trump and Reza Pahlavi. The movement has become increasingly visible in Republican politics, with Pahlavi positioning himself as a "transitional leader" for Iran. However, critics argue that the war being l

enriched_articles.title: Caught in the Crackdown: As Arrests at Anti-ICE Protests Piled Up, Prosecutions Crumbled
enriched_articles.text: A joint ProPublica and FRONTLINE investigation reveals that over 300 protesters and bystanders arrested during Trump administration immigration sweeps have seen charges frequently dropped or lost. Federal agents have routinely accused dissenters of being domestic terrorists, but more than a third of cases have crumbled under scrutiny. The investigation documents aggressive tactics including tear gas and rubber bullets, with prosecutors often dismissing charges when video evidence contradicts off

enriched_articles.title: The role of literature as the key to personal freedom
enriched_articles.text: Marcel Proust defended literature not as moral instruction but as a unique form of communication that allows readers to connect with great minds on their own terms. Unlike social interactions constrained by social conventions, reading offers complete freedom to engage with texts according to one's own purposes. The essay explores how literature serves as a "fruitful miracle of communication in the midst of solitude," allowing readers to expand their experience beyond measure and access immortali

enriched_articles.title: Nuclear Armageddon: U.S., Russia, China battling collective amnesia, says UN chief Guterres
enriched_articles.text: UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres warned at the NPT Review Conference that global military spending has soared to $2.7 trillion - thirteen times all development aid and equivalent to Africa's entire GDP. He stated that nuclear weapons make no one safer and that the world is experiencing "collective amnesia" about nuclear dangers. Guterres called for renewed commitment to disarmament and warned that new technologies like AI and quantum computing compound nuclear threats.

enriched_articles.title: Trump 'not satisfied' with Iran's latest proposal to reopen Strait Hormuz, end the war
enriched_articles.text: President Trump rejected Iran's latest proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and end the war, as the proposal did not address Iran's nuclear program. The plan would have set aside nuclear discussions until after the war ends and shipping disputes are resolved. Meanwhile, Iran's Foreign Minister blamed the US for failed talks during meetings with Putin in Russia, and several ASEAN countries are increasingly turning to Russian oil amid regional energy pressures from the Hormuz closure.
